name: Recruitment-Criteria Stringency and Eligibility Bandwidth
consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission clinical screening (e.g., mandatory recent polysomnography, collateral history only from bed partners) reduces enrollment variance but increases rejection rate and delays time-to-first-participant. Systemic: Narrower inclusion bands (e.g., NREM parasomnia frequency 3–10 episodes/month) improve event-capture yield to ~85% per participant and reduce time-to-10-events by ~3 weeks per stay, but may exclude genuine phenotypes and shrink addressable population by 20–30%. Strategic: Tighter criteria accelerate methods publication and Aim 2 completion but risk missing heterogeneity in age, comorbidity, or medication exposure that a less stringent cohort would reveal—a trade-off between publication speed and phenotypic breadth.
options: Enforce mandatory specialist sleep medicine physician pre-admission interview, prior polysomnography result within 2 years, and bed-partner/household-member collateral history for all candidates, using a formal clinical vetting score ≥8/10 to admit, resulting in ~60–65 enrolled participants with high event yield but potential 25–30% pre-screening rejection rate.

Adopt moderate gatekeeping requiring either prior polysomnography or a diagnostic interview plus structured questionnaire (e.g., ICSD-3 criteria checklist) without mandatory collateral history, targeting 70–80 enrolled participants with ~75% event-capture rate and more diverse phenotypes.

Relax pre-admission requirements to self-reported NREM parasomnia history validated via brief telephone screening only, accepting higher heterogeneity and potentially lower event yield (~65–70%) in exchange for enrollment of up to 90–100 participants, enabling a broader phenotypic registry and increased power for rare subgroup discovery.
review: Controls methodological rigor and reproducibility (strict criteria yield cleaner cohorts) vs. generalizability and phenotypic discovery (looser criteria capture population diversity). Weakness: The options fail to consider the temporal cost of case-by-case ethical review for high-risk exclusions (e.g., untreated apnea or wandering risk), which may impose unforeseen delays independent of intake stringency.

name: Sensing-Tier Deployment and Nightly Montage Escalation Protocol
consequences: Immediate: Deploying full PSG montage on all nights (versus scheduled enhanced nights plus escalation) eliminates data gaps and sensor-switch artifacts but increases technician annotation burden ~300% and raises equipment depreciation and power costs. Systemic: Scheduled PSG on nights 1, mid-stay, and final only (plus escalation post-event flagging) reduces annotation load by ~60% and keeps supplies costs ~€180K/year versus ~€280K/year for continuous PSG, enabling reinvestment in data engineering or participant retention incentives. Strategic: Sparse PSG creates risk that parasomnia morphology is systematically under-sampled on routine nights—a trade-off between operational sustainability and phenotypic completeness, especially if event clustering occurs between scheduled nights.
options: Deploy full clinical-grade PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, bilateral leg EMG, ECG, respiratory effort, SpO₂) every night for all 8 active suites, accepting ~€280K annual equipment and consumables cost, 1.5 additional FTE for real-time annotation, and risk of technician fatigue, in order to eliminate any systematic under-sampling of event morphology.

Implement the planned tiered model: low-burden dry-electrode EEG headband, mattress sensor, infrared camera, and ambient audio nightly; enhanced PSG montage on first, mid-stay, and final nights; escalation PSG (full montage) within 6 hours of any technician-flagged event, keeping annual consumables cost ~€180K and distributing annotation workload across routine review plus targeted escalation.

Adopt minimal nightly sensing (dry-electrode EEG headband and contact-free mattress sensor only, no camera or microphone) with full PSG escalation deployed within 4 hours of detected arousal or movement anomaly, reducing equipment footprint and privacy concerns but requiring highly sensitive automated alert algorithms and risking missed events that lack prominent polygraphic markers.
review: Controls data richness and artifact detection capability (full montage nightly) vs. operational scalability and cost efficiency (tiered escalation). Weakness: The options fail to consider seasonal or longer-term drift in sensor calibration (e.g., mattress sensors degrading over 3 years), which could systematically bias inter-participant or longitudinal comparisons if not explicitly monitored.

name: Night-Shift Staffing Model and Suite Coverage Ratio
consequences: Immediate: Expanding the rotating technician team from 3 to 4 staff ensures single-technician coverage per 2 suites (rather than 2.67 suites) on every shift, reducing cognitive load per event and enabling better real-time annotation, but adds ~€280K/year salary cost. Systemic: Lower suite-per-technician ratio decreases mean response time to flagged events from ~12 minutes to ~5 minutes and reduces annotation errors by ~15–20% (per error-rate studies in polysomnography labs), but forces either deferred expansion to 12 suites or reallocation of €280K from participant compensation, data storage, or algorithm development. Strategic: Tighter staffing improves safety and data quality but locks €280K annually into payroll, preventing pivot toward algorithm acceleration or extended-stay incentives—a trade-off between immediate safety/quality and mid-project research flexibility.
options: Retain the planned 3-technician rotating model (8 suites, ~2.67 suites per technician per shift), accepting mean event-response latency of 10–12 minutes and potential annotation bottlenecks during high-activity nights, but preserving €280K/year for reinvestment in computational tools or participant retention bonuses.

Hire a 4th research technician on a 0.8 FTE permanent overnight contract, achieving single-technician coverage of 2 suites per shift and mean response latency of ~5 minutes, consuming the €280K but improving annotated event quality and enabling safer expansion toward 10–11 active suites by year 3.

Implement a hybrid model with 3 core technicians plus a 4th on-call technician (paid per-shift) activated on nights when more than 4 suites are occupied, maintaining base budget while scaling coverage flexibly and delaying formal 4th-technician hire until occupation data justify it.
review: Controls immediate response capability and annotation reliability (larger team) vs. budget flexibility and research agility (lean team). Weakness: The options fail to consider technician burnout and turnover risk in overnight-shift roles, which may inflate true employment cost via recruitment, training, and knowledge loss beyond salary lines.

name: Extended-Stay Protocol and Event-Capture Ceiling
consequences: Immediate: Offering automatic 2-week extensions to any participant with zero captured events (capped at 10 weeks total) increases mean time-in-facility to ~6 weeks and per-participant cost to ~€4,200 (at €80/night plus meals), but captures an additional ~15–20% of typically silent participants and reduces protocol-exit without event-capture data. Systemic: Extension protocol adds ~80–100 participant-nights per year and ~€7K–€8.5K annual participant compensation but may improve event-capture yield from 75% to 88–90%, yielding ~8–12 additional adjudicated events/year and shortening time-to-minimum-event threshold for Aim 2 by ~6–8 weeks. Strategic: Longer stays risk participant fatigue, weekend-effect artifact (when spouses visit or sleep patterns shift), and reduced protocol compliance—a trade-off between event density and data fidelity, particularly for participants with genuinely infrequent episodes who may resent forced extension without activity.
options: Offer no automatic extensions; discharge any participant after 8 weeks without captured events, minimizing per-participant cost and facility occupancy volatility but accepting ~20–25% unproductive admissions per the protocol ceiling and deferring rare-episode phenotyping to a future phase-two cohort.

Implement the planned extension protocol: automatic 2-week extension for zero-event participants up to 10 weeks total, with a mid-extension psychologist check-in and optional early discharge, targeting 88–90% event-capture yield and accepting ~€7K–€8.5K additional annual cost as a trade-off for more complete phenotyping.

Offer selective extensions only to participants who meet predefined risk factors for low yield (e.g., age >55, medication use, sporadic episode history), reserving extension budget for high-potential recruits and capping total extended-stay cohort at 10 participants/year, balancing targeted resource use with flexibility.
review: Controls data completeness for rare-episode phenotypes (liberal extensions) vs. operational efficiency and participant experience (time-limited stays). Weakness: The options fail to consider the confounding of sleep-environment adaptation—participants may require 3–5 nights to reach baseline sleep architecture, meaning event absence in weeks 1–2 may reflect acclimatization rather than genuine rarity, and extension protocols may systematically bias toward participants who do adapt.

name: Data Sharing Scope and Repository Deposition Constraints
consequences: Immediate: Restricting public data release to de-identified physiological and sensor data (excluding video) under data use agreements limits reuse potential and secondary research but eliminates privacy litigation and regulatory risk. Systemic: Depositing polysomnography signals, arousal/movement annotations, and environmental sensor streams in a BIDS-compatible public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, NARPS) enables ~20–30 secondary research projects per 100-participant cohort within 5 years and establishes the unit as a resource, but requires 6–9 additional months of de-identification validation and metadata curation (€40K–€60K cost). Strategic: Open data sharing accelerates field progress and drives citations but locks in phenotypic categories and artifact-flagging decisions made during initial scoring, risking replication constraints if later raters or algorithms identify systematic biases in the original annotation—a trade-off between immediate generosity and long-term methodological transparency.
options: Restrict all data (including de-identified polysomnography and sensor streams) to University of Bonn collaborators and DFG-funded partners under institutional data use agreements, retaining publication priority and methodological control, but sacrificing external validation and secondary research uptake.

Deposit de-identified polysomnography and environmental sensor data (no video) in a public BIDS-compatible repository (OpenNeuro or equivalent) within 12 months of study completion, with participant metadata (age, episode frequency, medication) removed, enabling secondary research and external algorithm benchmarking while preserving privacy and requiring ~€50K curation effort.

Release a staged public dataset: polysomnography signals and arousal annotations at 24 months post-launch (mid-project), environmental sensors and movement data at study close, and withhold bedroom video and clinical identifiers indefinitely, staggering reproducibility and managing reputational risk incrementally.
review: Controls privacy protection and institutional intellectual property (restricted sharing) vs. scientific transparency and field impact (open sharing). Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk that early public release of de-identified data may enable inference of participant identity through longitudinal tracking or linkage with health records, particularly in a 3-year cohort where phenotypic detail is high and the eligible population is relatively small.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development Scope and Validation Target
consequences: Immediate: Developing a semi-automated event-triage tool that ranks probable parasomnia episodes (sensitivity/specificity-focused) requires ~€150K developer time and machine-learning infrastructure but reduces technician review burden by ~35–45%, reallocating ~0.5 FTE capacity to Aims 1 and 2. Systemic: Benchmarking the triage model against dual human scorer agreement (rather than claiming autonomous diagnosis) keeps validation scope realistic and publishable within 30 months, but limits clinical claim and pharma-partner interest to ~25–30% versus an autonomous-diagnosis claim. Strategic: Conservative algorithm scope (ranking candidate events rather than autonomous detection) avoids overfitting to training data and maintains credibility for phase-two work but may disappoint stakeholders expecting a deployable diagnostic tool—a trade-off between methodological humility and commercial/translational appeal.
options: Develop a fully supervised machine-learning classifier (random forest or gradient boosting on polysomnography features and movement amplitude) trained to detect parasomnia versus normal nocturnal movement with reported sensitivity ≥95%, specificity ≥90%, and marketed as a diagnostic aid, accepting risk of overfit to training cohort and potential regulatory scrutiny under IVD/MDR frameworks.

Implement a semi-automated event-triage system using unsupervised clustering and anomaly-detection algorithms (isolation forests or autoencoders) to rank polysomnography segments by probability of parasomnia relevance, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement with reported precision/recall at top-k thresholds (e.g., recall ≥80% at top 20% ranked), positioning output as decision-support rather than diagnosis.

Create a lightweight rule-based triage heuristic (e.g., flagging arousal+leg EMG bursts exceeding parameterized thresholds) requiring minimal training data and computational overhead, validated against human annotation, enabling rapid iteration and transparent criteria but sacrificing predictive power and generalization to rare phenotypes.
review: Controls algorithmic sophistication and potential translational value (supervised learning) vs. transparency, generalizability, and honest validation (semi-supervised/rule-based). Weakness: The options fail to consider the data-distribution shift between training (Bonn cohort) and deployment (other sleep labs or home settings), which may degrade algorithm performance by 10–20% and is rarely quantified in published sleep medicine classifiers.

name: External Advisory Board Authority and Milestone-Triggered Program Review
consequences: Immediate: Establishing a 3-member external scientific advisory board (SAB) that reviews progress every 6 months and holds veto authority over budget reallocations >€50K ensures credible governance but introduces 2–3 weeks latency into operational decisions and risks mission-drift if board members prioritize methodological purity over practical facility sustainability. Systemic: Hard failure triggers at month 18 (enrollment <25 participants or <40 adjudicated events) mandate external program review, potentially triggering protocol revision or early closure; compliance improves recruitment strategy fidelity and data quality but may force uncomfortable mid-project pivots (e.g., relaxing eligibility) that damage scientific narrative consistency. Strategic: Independent oversight builds funder and ethics-board confidence and legitimizes results for publication, but formal trigger-based reviews can become administratively adversarial rather than supportive—a trade-off between accountability and collaborative problem-solving momentum.
options: Vest full operational and budget authority in the PI with SAB serving in advisory-only capacity (no veto, monthly teleconference summaries), accelerating decisions and preserving autonomy but risking mission-drift and reviewer skepticism about independence.

Implement the planned governance: SAB with veto authority over reallocations >€50K, 6-month reviews, and formal month-18 failure triggers (enrollment <25, events <40) initiating mandatory external review; if triggers are met, conduct root-cause analysis and present revised protocol within 8 weeks, accepting decision latency in exchange for demonstrable accountability.

Establish a lightweight SAB with advisory authority only and contingent-trigger reviews: SAB formally reviews program only if PI self-reports concerns about trajectory (e.g., enrollment <30 by month 14) or if funder requests it, enabling flexibility and collaborative problem-solving while preserving SAB credibility as escalation mechanism.
review: Controls funder/ethics-board accountability and methodology rigor (strong board authority) vs. operational agility and PI autonomy (advisory-only). Weakness: The options fail to consider the composition and incentive structure of the SAB itself—if board members are prominent sleep researchers with competing facilities, conflicts of interest may bias them toward competitive rather than cooperative advice, particularly if Bonn's unit threatens their grant landscape.

name: Naturalism-versus-Clinical-Fidelity Trade-off and Base Montage Configuration
consequences: Immediate: Choosing lighter baseline sensing (dry-electrode headbands, mattress, infrared) on routine nights reduces setup friction and participant acclimation time, but sacrifices EOG and chin EMG fidelity on non-scheduled nights. Systemic: A lean baseline configuration could reduce nightly technician setup burden by 30–40 minutes, permitting higher participant density per technician and lower staffing costs per participant (~€12K annually per slot), but will generate 15–25% more false-positive movement flagging requiring manual review, increasing annotation workload. Strategic: This decision determines whether the facility operates as a high-throughput phenotyping center (accepting some physiological ambiguity) or a lower-volume ultra-high-resolution unit; it directly shapes publication timeline, replicability claims, and competitiveness for multi-site parasomnia consortia recruitment.
options: Retain the three-tier model (routine dry sensors, scheduled enhanced PSG, escalation full PSG) as specified, accepting that most events lack standard EEG/EOG/EMG resolution and relying on algorithmic and behavioral inference for phenotyping.

Deploy intermediate PSG (standard scalp EEG plus chin EMG only, omitting EOG and leg EMG) on all routine nights, reducing acclimation artifacts and false-alarm burden at the cost of ~€120K in additional annual equipment and consumables and ~8 hours weekly of technician setup time.

Install full PSG montage on every participant on every night from day one, guaranteeing clinical-grade resolution and eliminating any data-quality asymmetry, but requiring three additional night technicians, raising annual personnel costs by €180K and extending recruitment timelines as the facility reaches capacity immediately.
review: Controls clinical rigor vs. operational scalability. Weakness: The options fail to consider how sensor selection affects participant comfort and retention—a full montage every night may produce higher dropout rates, especially in the 2–8 week stay window, which undermines longitudinal trend detection.

name: Technician Event-Escalation Authority and Real-Time Decision Autonomy
consequences: Immediate: Granting night technicians explicit decision authority to trigger escalation PSG recording after observing motor events or arousal clusters (rather than waiting for PI review the next morning) enables immediate clinical-grade capture when participants are still in an active behavioral state. Systemic: Technician autonomy could increase captured event-escalation recordings by 40–60%, raising the event yield per participant from an estimated 0.8–1.2 to 1.3–2.0 adjudicated events per stay, substantially shortening the timeline to Aim 2 phenotyping sufficiency. Strategic: This lever determines whether the facility can reach publication-ready event density within the 3-year window or requires grant extension; it also exposes the team to technician variability in judgment (false-positive escalations waste PSG resources) and liability concerns if escalation decisions lack clear algorithmic anchoring.
options: Retain centralized escalation authority: technicians flag candidate events in real time via annotation software, but the on-call PI or senior postdoc must approve escalation PSG deployment within 15 minutes, ensuring expert oversight and consistent criteria application.

Implement a hybrid semi-autonomous model: technicians may trigger escalation PSG immediately if objective criteria are met (e.g., three or more discrete motor bursts within 10 minutes, plus arousal marker, detected via software automation), with morning retrospective audit by the PI to refine decision thresholds.

Delegate full escalation authority to trained night technicians with monthly competency recertification: technicians independently decide when to deploy escalation PSG based on written decision trees, reducing response latency and PI availability constraints, though introducing technician-dependent variability and potential over-escalation.
review: Controls PI oversight vs. event-capture velocity. Weakness: The options fail to consider how escalation autonomy interacts with technician fatigue and shift length—night shifts spanning 8+ hours may degrade judgment quality late in the shift, potentially increasing false-positive escalations regardless of authority structure.

name: Phenotyping Scope Expansion and Secondary Analysis Ambition
consequences: Immediate: Restricting the research program strictly to NREM parasomnia characterization (sleepwalking, confusional arousals, sleep terrors) and REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol minimizes scope creep and keeps the team focused on Aims 1–3. Systemic: A narrow phenotyping mandate could allow publication of Aim 2 results (longitudinal characterization and morphology) by month 30, enabling early career advancement for trainees and supporting a phase-two grant application with proof-of-concept data, but forgoes high-impact secondary avenues (e.g., seizure-parasomnia mimicry, HLA-DQ B1*0602 associations with narcolepsy-cataplexy overlap, treatment response trajectories). Strategic: This decision shapes the facility's scientific reputation and long-term funding trajectory—narrow focus accelerates initial publications but may limit visibility within the broader sleep neuroscience community; expanded scope delays publications but increases citation impact and attracts multi-site consortium interest.
options: Maintain strict focus on parasomnia characterization per the current Aims 1–3, with RBD as a separate protocol, deferring all secondary phenotyping (metabolic, genetic, neuroimaging correlates, medication response) to a phase-two proposal.

Negotiate with the DFG and University of Bonn for a formal supplemental budget (€200–300K) to add structured biosampling (saliva for genetic screening, basic bloodwork for metabolic screening) to all baseline visits, enabling post-hoc genetic and inflammatory marker analysis without disrupting the residential monitoring protocol.

Expand the study population to include narcolepsy-cataplexy patients and patients with sleep-related hypoventilation as comparative phenotypes for multi-modal nocturnal motor pattern analysis, requiring a revised ethics approval but substantially increasing publication scope and consortium attractiveness at the cost of recruitment complexity and extended timeline.
review: Controls scientific depth vs. timeline velocity. Weakness: The options fail to consider how secondary analyses depend on external collaboration (genetic labs, imaging centers) that may introduce delays and dependency on third-party turnaround times, undermining the facility's ability to meet publication deadlines independently.

name: Annotation Labor Model and Human-Versus-Algorithmic Scorer Balance
consequences: Immediate: Relying on dual independent human scorers (the night technician annotation plus two independent raters per the current plan) for all events guarantees AASM-compliant phenotyping but requires ~600–800 hours of expert scoring time per year, consuming approximately 35–40% of one postdoctoral researcher's time or €70–90K in contracted scoring labor. Systemic: A full-human annotation pipeline could report inter-rater reliability (κ or ICC) of 0.80–0.92 by year two, establishing methodological credibility, but delays secondary analysis by 3–6 months per cohort cycle and constrains the sample size analyzable per publication cycle (approximately 40–60 events per 6-month cycle). Strategic: This decision directly affects publication velocity, replicability claims, and the team's ability to benchmark algorithmic event-triage tools—if human annotation capacity is saturated, algorithm development stalls behind phenotyping.
options: Maintain the dual-rater gold standard for all events, accepting that annotation throughput caps event-based publications at 2–3 per year and that algorithm benchmarking occurs only after year-two event accumulation.

Implement a hybrid tiered-confidence model: all events receive initial dual-human scoring; high-confidence events (κ ≥ 0.85 across raters) are considered gold-standard phenotyping, while borderline events (κ 0.60–0.84) are jointly rescored by both raters with adjudication, reducing repeat scoring by ~25% and accelerating secondary analysis entry.

Develop and validate a rule-based or machine-learning event-detection pre-filter using the first 100 annotated participant records, deployed to automatically score 30–40% of candidate events as 'probable event', 'ambiguous', or 'non-event' before human review, with humans scoring only ambiguous and machine-flagged outliers, cutting human annotation burden by ~45% by year two.
review: Controls human-expert oversight vs. labor scalability. Weakness: The options fail to consider that any algorithmic pre-filtering introduces systematic bias if trained on incomplete data (e.g., early cohorts may differ in acclimation artifacts, sensor saturation, or technician experience), potentially skewing phenotyping estimates for later participants.

name: Regulatory Pathway Selectivity and Ethics-Approval Scope Binding
consequences: Immediate: Obtaining a single consolidated ethics approval for NREM parasomnias and RBD under one protocol, rather than maintaining separate approvals, reduces administrative overhead and shortens the approval cycle by 3–4 months at launch. Systemic: A unified ethics pathway could accelerate first-participant enrollment by month 6 instead of month 9, yielding 20–25 additional participant-nights of data by the end of year one (~€35–50K value in sensor coverage and staff utilization). Strategic: Conversely, a unified approval creates governance inflexibility—if RBD recruitment substantially underperforms or neurologic screening reveals safety concerns specific to RBD, the entire protocol may require amendment, triggering re-review and delaying NREM cohort progression; separate protocols preserve independence but double ethics and safety-reporting burden.
options: File a single consolidated protocol for NREM and RBD with the University of Bonn ethics committee, accepting that any protocol modification affecting either condition requires full re-review and that safety reporting must cover both cohorts conjointly.

Maintain separate ethics approvals (NREM primary protocol, RBD as an independent protocol under the separate neurologic screening pathway), accepting 4–6 weeks additional review time and duplicated safety-reporting burden but preserving the ability to pause RBD recruitment independently if needed.

Propose a multi-site ethics amendment under the German DFG framework that positions the Bonn unit as a node in a multi-center parasomnia consortium (e.g., coordinated with sleep labs in Zurich and Munich), centralizing ethics review via a lead institutional review board and reducing per-site approval time by 2–3 weeks, though requiring consortium coordination infrastructure.
review: Controls administrative efficiency vs. operational independence. Weakness: The options fail to consider that harmonizing ethics approvals across multi-site consortia introduces delays if any single site encounters protocol violations or safety concerns, potentially halting progress at all sites even if the incident is site-specific.

name: Publication Strategy Timing and Dataset Maturity Threshold
consequences: Immediate: Publishing methods-validation and preliminary phenotyping results by month 24 (after enrollment of ~40–45 participants and capture of ~50–70 events) establishes early scientific credibility and supports a phase-two grant application while the study is still enrolling. Systemic: Early publication (month 24–26) could generate 3–5 citations per paper within 18 months, positioning trainees for postdoctoral placement and signaling competence to the sleep medicine community, but risks incomplete longitudinal characterization (only 30–50% of the target 50–70 enrollment complete) and premature claims about event-triage algorithm performance if validation against the control cohort is incomplete. Strategic: Delayed publication (month 32–36, after full cohort completion) ensures comprehensive phenotyping, full algorithm benchmarking, and stronger evidence for intervention mechanistic hypotheses in a phase-two proposal, but concedes priority to competing groups and may arrive too late for high-impact 2026–2027 consortium funding cycles.
options: Target preliminary publication by month 26 on the basis of 40–45 NREM participants and captured events, focusing on methods validation, facility safety/operational metrics, and initial morphology descriptors, explicitly stating cohort incompleteness and deferring phenotyping claims to the final dataset.

Implement a rolling-publication strategy with three synchronized submissions: month 22 for methods/operations, month 28 for event morphology and longitudinal clustering (intermediate cohort), and month 36 for comprehensive phenotyping plus algorithm benchmarking, reducing citation latency without premature claims.

Consolidate all results into two high-impact joint submissions at month 34–36 (one for longitudinal characterization and phenotyping, one for algorithm development and validation), targeting top-tier sleep medicine or neurology journals and accepting delayed visibility in exchange for impact and comprehensiveness.
review: Controls scientific credibility vs. publication velocity. Weakness: The options fail to consider that early publication may attract early critical scrutiny if phenotyping results are incomplete—rival groups may design faster studies targeting the same questions with more complete datasets, potentially pre-empting the facility's leadership in parasomnia phenotyping.

name: Facility Expansion Trigger Conditions and 12-Suite Buildout Sequencing
consequences: Immediate: The current plan defers expansion to 12 suites until pilot data (months 12–18) prove acceptable data quality, false-alarm rates, staffing safety, and annotation throughput. Systemic: Waiting for validation before expansion ensures capital efficiency (~€400K saved if expansion is cancelled), but delays reaching full capacity until month 24–27, reducing total participant volume over the 3-year window by 30–40 participants (~€240–320K in lost sampling intensity and €100–150K in delayed publications). Strategic: Accelerating expansion to 12 suites by month 12 (before validation is complete) increases operational and financial risk—if the pilot reveals persistent false-alarm rates or unsafe staffing ratios, overheads scale without corrective capacity—but enables enrollment of 70–85 participants over 3 years, substantially strengthening phenotyping power and increasing likelihood of multi-site consortium recruitment.
options: Adhere to the conditional phased expansion: expand to 12 suites only after month 18 pilot data explicitly demonstrate ≥70% data-quality acceptance, ≤20% false-alarm escalation rates, safe staffing ratios, and manageable annotation throughput per technician.

Execute early expansion by month 12 contingent on enrollment reaching 20 participants and preliminary safety sign-off from the external advisory board, accepting higher financial and operational risk in exchange for 25–30 additional participant-study slots over the remaining 24 months.

Propose a hybrid flexible expansion: maintain 8 suites as the base and add modular temporary 'pop-up' suites (3–4 portable sleep cabins with full sensor infrastructure) on demand if enrollment outpaces capacity, allowing rapid scaling without capital commitment and enabling scale-down if recruitment falters.
review: Controls capital efficiency vs. enrollment velocity. Weakness: The options fail to consider that expanding to 12 suites also requires hiring 1–2 additional night technicians (€60–80K annually) regardless of participant utilization, creating fixed-cost drag if enrollment growth stalls.

name: Baseline Sensor Validation Rigor and Dry-Electrode Calibration Protocol
consequences: Immediate: Pre-admission dry-electrode headband calibration against scalp-placement gold standard determines signal fidelity before first night enrollment. → Systemic: A robust calibration step that requires 2–4 additional hours per participant reduces first-week false alarm rates by an estimated 15–25% and avoids re-admissions due to sensor malfunction, preserving the €80 per-night budget allocation. → Strategic: Early validation confidence enables confident reliance on nightly montage reduction and justifies the cost of escalation PSG only post-technician flag, but delays individual participant start dates and concentrates workload during intake weeks, creating tension between enrollment velocity and monitoring quality.
options: Conduct comprehensive pre-admission dry-electrode fitting and 2-hour baseline recording in the outpatient sleep clinic 1–2 weeks prior to residential stay, with dual technician sign-off on impedance, artifact patterns, and signal morphology before participant arrival.

Perform abbreviated 30-minute headband fitting on arrival day with same-evening 3-hour baseline recording in-unit, followed by technician review first morning and re-calibration if impedance or artifact exceeds threshold, allowing same-day or next-day escalation to full PSG if signal quality is marginal.

Omit pre-admission calibration and conduct fitting entirely on night 1 with real-time supervised electrode adjustment by night technician, accepting higher first-night artifact burden and deferring full PSG escalation decision to night 2 if signal is non-diagnostic, trading intake convenience for potential loss of one baseline night.
review: Controls signal-quality assurance vs. participant enrollment throughput. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether dry-electrode headband specifications (impedance tolerance, age of device) are already validated in the NREM parasomnia population versus requiring local optimization.

name: Matched Control Recruitment Timing and Cohort Sequencing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Control participants (normal sleepers undergoing identical residential monitoring) must provide baseline false-positive sensor rates and nocturnal movement templates for event-triage algorithm training and validation. → Systemic: If controls are recruited as a parallel parallel stream, an estimated 15–20 control admissions are needed, consuming 15–20 suite-weeks and 2–3 staff FTE effort; if deferred to year 2 after case phenotyping is complete, algorithm development accelerates but validation is delayed by 6+ months and publishing timelines slip. → Strategic: Early control enrollment builds algorithm generalization capacity but stretches year-1 budget and staffing; deferred controls reduce near-term risk but couple algorithm maturation to later-phase funding and create pressure to publish preliminary findings without external benchmark.
options: Stagger matched controls throughout year 1 and year 2, admitting one control participant per month in even-numbered suite slots, ensuring algorithm training data and false-positive benchmarking run in parallel with case collection and minimizing later-stage model retraining.

Defer all control admissions to months 13–20 in year 2, once case enrollment has stabilized and preliminary parasomnia phenotypes are characterized, allowing algorithm development on case data alone during year 1 and controls to serve purely as external validation cohort.

Enroll a small control pilot (n=5) in months 6–9 of year 1 to validate sensor baseline signals and refine false-alarm thresholds operationally, then scale to full matched cohort (n=15) in year 2 in parallel with ongoing case recruitment.
review: Controls algorithm-training robustness vs. near-term operational bandwidth. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether the phenotypic heterogeneity within NREM parasomnias (sleepwalking vs. confusional arousals vs. sleep terrors) requires subtype-matched controls or whether a single normal-sleep control group suffices for triage algorithm purposes.

name: Enhanced-Night PSG Montage Footprint and Electrode Burden Escalation
consequences: Immediate: Enhanced nights (first, mid-stay, final) deploy full clinical PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) capturing richer motor and cortical signatures than dry-electrode baseline. → Systemic: Full montage adds approximately 45 minutes setup time, €120 in consumables per night, and technician workload of ~2 hours per participant; spreading 50–70 participants across enhanced nights at three per participant yields 150–210 enhanced-night slots per year, consuming 2–3 additional FTE or requiring external contract polysomnography services at €300–500 per night, a cost delta of roughly €18K–35K annually. → Strategic: Richer data enables morphological subtyping and supports mechanistic publications, but outsourcing PSG introduces external dependency and quality variance; conversely, in-housing PSG requires hiring or retraining technicians in standard EEG placement, increasing staffing complexity and deferring focus from real-time event monitoring.
options: Conduct all enhanced PSG nights in-house using the existing three technicians cross-trained on standard scalp electrode placement, with one technician assigned to PSG setup 2 hours before each enhanced night and the others maintaining real-time event monitoring, accepting occasional setup delays and compressed annotation time on high-load nights.

Contract with University Hospital Bonn's sleep laboratory for external PSG acquisition on enhanced nights, with hospital technicians performing electrode placement and recordings transferred nightly to the residential unit for synchronization and technician annotation, trading in-house training burden for external quality assurance and schedule flexibility.

Limit enhanced-night PSG to first and final nights only (eliminating mid-stay montage), reducing PSG footprint by 33%, and use nightly dry-electrode escalation protocol for any technician-flagged events during mid-stay, reserving full montage for bookend clinical characterization and reducing consumables and scheduling friction.
review: Controls morphological-phenotyping richness vs. operational staffing feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether external PSG setup introduces data-synchronization or quality-control risks that undermine the benefits of outsourcing, or whether mid-stay PSG nights are scientifically justified by event capture rates observed during year-1 pilots.

name: Technician Real-Time Annotation Authority and Event-Flag Consensus Threshold
consequences: Immediate: Night technicians must flag putative parasomnia events in real time to trigger escalation PSG and inform participant safety decisions; flagging authority (one technician's judgment vs. mandatory peer confirmation) determines response speed and safety margin. → Systemic: Unilateral technician flagging enables sub-5-minute escalation response but risks over-escalation, consuming PSG capacity for false positives and increasing per-participant cost by an estimated 10–15%; requiring dual-technician consensus before escalation reduces false-positive PSG deployments by approximately 20–30% but delays response by 10–15 minutes and may miss brief, borderline events, creating liability if an unflagged event results in injury. → Strategic: Rapid unilateral flagging prioritizes participant safety and operational responsiveness but inflates costs and algorithm-training noise; consensus-based flagging increases analytical specificity but introduces coordination overhead on small night teams and creates pressure to supplement with automated triage algorithms earlier than planned.
options: Authorize each night technician to independently flag and escalate full PSG following event recognition, with documented real-time notes uploaded immediately, accepting escalation frequency and cost overrun in exchange for unambiguous safety priority and rapid data acquisition on every suspected event.

Require two-technician consensus on event flags only when both are actively monitoring the same suite (standard during multi-participant nights), and permit unilateral flags if only one technician is active or if the flagging technician judges imminent safety risk, balancing responsiveness with over-escalation control.

Implement a standardized 2-minute observation window: technician observes the putative event, documents objective criteria (movement amplitude, respiratory rate, vocalizations) in a structured form, and flags only if three or more AASM morphology criteria are met before triggering PSG escalation, reducing false-positive flags by pre-filtering on clinical features.
review: Controls safety-response velocity vs. PSG resource utilization. Weakness: The options fail to consider the technician fatigue, attention decay, and inter-rater reliability during long night shifts, or whether a standardized morphology checklist is feasible to apply in real time without significantly delaying response.

name: Secondary RBD Protocol Separation and Neurologic Screening Apparatus
consequences: Immediate: RBD (REM sleep behavior disorder) is included in the plan as a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening due to its distinct clinical trajectory, requiring an additional clinical interview, neurological examination, and neurology faculty adjudication before study entry to exclude Parkinson's disease and other synucleinopathies. → Systemic: Dedicated RBD screening adds approximately 1–2 hours per RBD participant (interview + neuro exam + documentation), requiring collaboration with the Department of Neurology and consuming roughly 50–100 clinical-interview-hours over three years if RBD enrollment reaches 10–15 participants; this adds approximately €8K–12K in external neurologist fees or diverts senior clinical staff effort away from NREM management. → Strategic: Rigorous RBD adjudication strengthens scientific credibility and publication impact because RBD's neurobiological links to neurodegeneration are well-established, but the separate pathway increases gatekeeping complexity and may discourage RBD referrals if screening is perceived as burdensome; conversely, streamlining RBD intake into the main pathway risks misclassification and contaminates NREM-specific phenotyping.
options: Establish a dedicated RBD fast-track pathway with a single neurology co-investigator from the Department of Neurology conducting in-person neurologic screening within 2 weeks of referral in the clinical setting, with results fed to the sleep team before final enrollment decision, maintaining scientific rigor but adding 2–3 weeks to RBD enrollment timelines.

Embed a structured neurologic examination questionnaire (gait, tremor, cognition screening items) into the residential unit's first-night clinical assessment, to be reviewed by the PI (sleep physician) with neurology consultation only if red flags emerge, reducing upfront neurology bottleneck and allowing faster RBD enrollment but accepting lower sensitivity for early neurodegeneration clues.

Defer RBD enrollment to year 2 after NREM operations are stable, allowing year-1 focus on NREM parasomnia characterization and postponing the additional neurologic screening infrastructure until sufficient operational experience exists, trading RBD sample size for simplification of year-1 launch and enabling year-2 RBD recruitment to benefit from refined protocols.
review: Controls RBD scientific rigor vs. recruitment and operational simplification. Weakness: The options fail to consider whether the expected RBD event-capture rate (likely lower than NREM parasomnias due to REM-sleep specificity) justifies the additional screening investment, or whether the residential stay duration for RBD may need to be longer to capture sufficient REM-sleep episodes.

name: Annotation Rater Independence Verification and Dual-Scorer Workflow Topology
consequences: Immediate: All events are scored independently by two raters using AASM criteria, with inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) reported; workflow topology (simultaneous dual-scoring vs. sequential adjudication vs. staggered shifts) determines annotation bottleneck and re-review burden. → Systemic: Simultaneous dual-scoring (both raters score identical nights in parallel) requires two independent scoring FTE and ensures unbiased inter-rater measurement but doubles annotation labor costs by approximately €40K–60K over three years; sequential adjudication (rater 1 scores, rater 2 reviews only disagreements) reduces costs by 35–45% but risks biasing rater 2 toward rater 1's initial judgment and complicates kappa calculation. → Strategic: Parallel dual-scoring maximizes statistical rigor and publishability but strains budget and may require external contract raters; sequential review economizes costs but introduces dependency on algorithm-driven pre-filtering to focus rater 2 on high-ambiguity cases, coupling annotation methodology to event-triage tool maturity in a way that complicates early-stage validation.
options: Employ two full-time independent scorers (or equivalent FTE through part-time contract raters) throughout year 1 and year 2, with both scoring identical nights in parallel on separate workstations without communication until scoring is complete, ensuring unbiased inter-rater agreement measurement and publishable kappa statistics but allocating approximately 0.5 FTE of the €2M personnel budget to annotation.

Deploy a single senior rater scoring all nights, with a second rater (part-time, 0.25 FTE) reviewing 20% of nights selected randomly and 100% of nights with any morphological ambiguity flagged by algorithmic pre-filtering, reducing dual-scoring overhead while maintaining spot-check reliability and adjusting kappa calculation to account for design imbalance.

Stagger rater assignments such that rater 1 scores all nights collected during months 1–12 and rater 2 scores all nights in months 13–24, then cross-validate a 30-night sample in month 25, eliminating simultaneous burden but accepting temporal confounding in rater-reliability estimates and deferring inter-rater metric to late in the project.
review: Controls annotation-cost burden vs. inter-rater reliability rigor. Weakness: The options fail to consider the learning curve and fatigue effects within each rater across hundreds of nights, or whether consensus guidelines (e.g., AASM morphology criteria for parasomnias) are sufficiently objective to expect high inter-rater agreement without extensive rater training and calibration sessions.

name: Publication Sequencing and Longitudinal Data-Release Cadence
consequences: Immediate: The facility generates three publication-ready product streams: methods validation (facility safety, sensor benchmarking), longitudinal phenotyping (parasomnia patterns, triggers, clustering), and event-triage algorithm benchmarking. → Systemic: Publishing methods-validation results at month 18–20 (after 12 months of stable operation and 40+ captured events) establishes credibility and attracts follow-on funding, but early publication exposes preliminary event-capture yields and sensor false-alarm rates to scrutiny, potentially inviting critical commentary if yields are low; deferring to month 30 after algorithm benchmarking allows a more integrated narrative but risks scooping by competitors and delays grant re-submissions for phase-two expansion. → Strategic: Rapid methods papers maximize time for pharma partnerships and phase-two grants but may over-claim stability if early operational issues resurface; deliberate deferral improves data maturity and narrative coherence but extends the timeline to demonstrable impact and increases retention risk if staff or collaborators depart before publication.
options: Publish a methods validation paper on facility design, sensor specifications, and operational safety outcomes by month 20, using the pilot cohort (15–20 participants, 30–50 captured events) to establish benchmark inter-rater reliability and false-alarm rates, accepting preliminary sample size and using it as a strong foundation for a follow-on phase-two grant proposal.

Target a single comprehensive longitudinal phenotyping and algorithm-benchmarking manuscript by month 30, integrating methods, cohort characterization (50–70 participants), event morphologies, and event-triage model performance in one high-impact submission, trading early visibility for scientific integration and full-cohort statistical power but accepting 6–12 month delay in establishing external credibility.

Implement staggered publication strategy with a methods brief (preprint at month 15, formal publication by month 20) on sensor validation and facility operations, followed by two phenotyping papers (months 25 and 32) on NREM and RBD cohorts separately, and a final algorithm-validation paper at month 36, maximizing publication count and grant-visibility cadence but requiring sustained writing effort and risking fragmentation of narrative coherence.
review: Controls credibility-establishment velocity vs. data-maturity and narrative integration. Weakness: The options fail to consider the pharmaceutical partner's interest in early publication windows (the contract specifies 30-day pre-pub review rights) and whether accelerated publication schedules might incentivize selective reporting or premature claims of algorithm utility.
